enrichment.short_description: Mataroa is a minimalist blogging platform that strips away themes, analytics and social features so writers can focus purely on publishing markdown posts.
enrichment.seo_title: Mataroa Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mataroa review 2026: a minimalist markdown blogging platform with newsletters and comments. See features, $9/year premium pricing, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mataroa is a minimalist blogging platform launched in 2020 by an independent developer as a deliberately simple alternative to full-featured blog software. It was inspired by Bear Blog and built around the philosophy that writing tools should get out of the way: there are no themes to configure, no built-in analytics, no algorithmic feeds, and no obtrusive subscribe popups. Writers compose posts in Markdown through a plain, distraction-free editor, and Mataroa renders each post on a clean, fast-loading static-feeling page. The platform is run as a small, sustainably-priced side project rather than a venture-backed company; the maintainer has publicly stated the service's monthly server costs are only a few euros, which is why pricing stays extremely low. Core free-tier features include unlimited blog posts, RSS feeds, drafts and scheduled posts, custom pages, syntax highlighting for code blocks, image uploads (capped at 1MB per image and 100MB total per account, with 100GB of annual bandwidth), a simple opt-in comment system that authors can moderate post by post, and an email newsletter feature that lets readers subscribe to receive new posts directly in their inbox as plain text emails. Every post can be exported at any time as Markdown files in a zip archive, reflecting Mataroa's strong stance against vendor lock-in. The paid Premium tier costs 9 US dollars per year and unlocks a custom domain (so a blog can be served from the author's own domain instead of a mataroa.blog subdomain) and automatic monthly email exports of all posts as a backup. There is no advertising, no tracking pixels, and no data resale; the company's own blog and marketing materials emphasize privacy and longevity over growth metrics. Mataroa targets individual writers, technical bloggers, hobbyists, and anyone who wants to publish quickly without wrestling with a CMS, choosing a theme, or worrying about SEO plugins. Its closest competitors are Bear Blog, Write.as, and to a lesser extent Ghost and micro.blog, all of which compete on simplicity and writer-first design rather than marketing or e-commerce features. Mataroa differentiates itself through its exceptionally low price point, plain-text export philosophy, integrated newsletter delivery at no extra cost on the free plan, and a codebase that is open source on GitHub, letting technically inclined users self-host or audit the platform. Because it deliberately omits analytics, A/B testing, monetization tools, and design customization, Mataroa is not intended for businesses running content marketing operations; it is built for individual voices who value permanence, portability, and minimal friction over growth tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mataroa is a blogging platform built around radical simplicity. Instead of competing on themes, plugins, or growth features, it focuses on making it as fast as possible to write a post in Markdown and publish it, with nothing standing between the writer and the page.

Launched in 2020 and maintained as a small independent project, Mataroa has become popular among developers, indie hackers and personal bloggers who are tired of heavyweight CMS platforms and want a tool that behaves more like a text editor than a marketing suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every post is written in Markdown and rendered on a clean, single-column page with no theme switching required. Writers get RSS feeds, drafts, scheduled publishing, custom static pages and syntax-highlighted code blocks out of the box.

A built-in email newsletter lets readers subscribe to receive new posts in their inbox as plain text, and a lightweight, author-moderated comment system is available without needing a third-party embed. All content can be exported as Markdown files at any time, so writers are never locked in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mataroa's free plan includes unlimited posts, RSS, the newsletter, comments and image hosting (up to 1MB per image and 100MB per account), which covers the needs of most individual bloggers.
The Premium plan costs 9 US dollars per year and adds a custom domain plus automatic monthly email backups of every post, making it one of the least expensive paid blogging tiers available anywhere.

features.title: Markdown-first editor
features.description: Posts are written and stored as Markdown, keeping content portable and free of proprietary formatting.

features.title: Email newsletter
features.description: Readers can subscribe to a blog as a newsletter and receive new posts as plain-text emails.

features.title: Author-moderated comments
features.description: A simple comment system lets blog authors accept or reject comments on a per-post basis.

features.title: RSS feeds
features.description: Every blog automatically generates an RSS feed for readers and feed aggregators.

features.title: Custom domain (Premium)
features.description: Premium subscribers can point their own domain at their Mataroa blog.

features.title: Scheduled and draft posts
features.description: Posts can be saved as drafts or scheduled for future publication.

features.title: Markdown export
features.description: All posts can be exported as a zip archive of Markdown files at any time, free or paid.

features.title: Custom pages and syntax highlighting
features.description: Writers can add static pages beyond the blog feed and use syntax-highlighted code blocks in posts.

pros: Extremely low cost, with a capable free tier and a 9 dollar per year premium plan
No themes or configuration overhead, so publishing a post takes minutes
Full Markdown export keeps content portable and avoids lock-in
No analytics, tracking or ad clutter, appealing to privacy-conscious writers
Built-in newsletter and comments remove the need for third-party embeds
cons: Very limited design customization compared to full CMS platforms
No native analytics, so writers cannot see traffic without external tools
No monetization, membership or paywall features
Image storage limits (1MB per image, 100MB per account) can be restrictive
Small, single-maintainer team means feature development is slow and support is informal

faqs.question: Is Mataroa free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. Mataroa offers a free plan with unlimited posts, RSS, newsletter and comments. A Premium plan costing 9 US dollars per year adds a custom domain and automatic email exports.

faqs.question: Can I use my own domain with Mataroa
faqs.answer: Custom domains are available on the Premium plan, which costs 9 US dollars per year.

faqs.question: Does Mataroa support Markdown
faqs.answer: Yes, all posts are written in Markdown, and the entire blog can be exported as Markdown files at any time.

faqs.question: Does Mataroa have analytics
faqs.answer: No. Mataroa intentionally omits built-in analytics to keep the platform simple and privacy-friendly.

faqs.question: Can readers subscribe to a Mataroa blog by email
faqs.answer: Yes, Mataroa includes a built-in newsletter feature so readers can subscribe and receive new posts as plain-text emails.

faqs.question: Who is Mataroa best suited for
faqs.answer: Individual writers, developers and hobby bloggers who want a fast, distraction-free way to publish without managing a full CMS.

use_cases.title: Personal and developer blogging
use_cases.description: Writers who want to publish thoughts, notes or technical posts quickly without configuring a theme or CMS.

use_cases.title: Newsletter-style publishing
use_cases.description: Bloggers who want readers to receive new posts directly by email without setting up a separate newsletter tool.

use_cases.title: Portable, lock-in-free archiving
use_cases.description: Writers who want their content stored in plain Markdown that can be exported and moved elsewhere at any time.

features: Image uploads up to 1MB each, 100MB total

enrichment.short_description: Material for MkDocs is a widely used open-source documentation theme and framework built on MkDocs that turns Markdown into fast, searchable, professionally designed documentation sites.
enrichment.seo_title: Material for MkDocs Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Material for MkDocs review 2026: the open-source documentation theme by squidfunk. Features, former Insiders tiers, now free for all, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Material for MkDocs is an open-source theme and framework for MkDocs, the Python-based static site generator, that turns Markdown files into polished, searchable documentation websites. It was created by Martin Donath, known by the GitHub handle squidfunk, and has grown into one of the most widely adopted documentation frameworks in the open-source world, used by projects such as FastAPI, Kubernetes-adjacent tools, and countless developer libraries. The project is hosted on GitHub, where it has accumulated tens of thousands of stars and hundreds of contributors. Technically, Material for MkDocs builds on top of MkDocs and Google's Material Design language, rendering documentation as a responsive static site with an in-browser search engine that works entirely client-side, requiring no external search service. It supports instant loading navigation, dark and light color schemes, code annotation, content tabs, admonitions, diagrams via Mermaid.js, and integration with versioning tools like mike so that multiple versions of a documentation set can be published and switched between without overwriting older releases. For years the project operated on a sponsorware model: core features were free and open source, while roughly two dozen advanced features (such as instant navigation, social cards, and offline search) were bundled into a paid Insiders edition, funded through GitHub Sponsors starting at 15 US dollars per month for individuals and higher tiers for organizations, with top tiers including a video call with the creator. In late 2025, the project shifted out of this sponsorware model: as of the 9.7.0 release, all previously Insiders-only features were folded into the free, open-source edition, effectively making Material for MkDocs free for everyone. Following that change, the project entered maintenance mode, meaning the maintainer continues to ship bug and security fixes but is directing new feature development toward a successor project called Zensical, built by the same team and designed to be compatible with existing mkdocs.yml configuration files. Material for MkDocs is aimed at open-source maintainers, developer tools companies, and technical writers who need a professional-looking, fast, and free documentation site without adopting a heavier platform like GitBook, Docusaurus, or ReadMe. Its main competitors include Docusaurus (Meta's React-based documentation framework), GitBook, ReadMe, Sphinx with the Read the Docs theme, and its own successor Zensical. Material for MkDocs differentiates itself through its Markdown-first workflow, zero-JavaScript-framework dependency for authors, strong out-of-the-box SEO and accessibility defaults, and a search experience that runs entirely in the browser without a backend service, making it especially attractive for teams that want static, easily hostable documentation with no server-side search infrastructure to maintain.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Material for MkDocs is a documentation framework that layers Google's Material Design language on top of MkDocs, letting teams turn a folder of Markdown files into a fast, professional documentation website with minimal configuration.

Created by developer Martin Donath, the project became one of the most popular open-source documentation tools, used by thousands of software projects for API references, developer guides and knowledge bases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The theme ships with a fully client-side search engine, instant page loading, light and dark color schemes, code annotations, content tabs, admonitions and diagram support via Mermaid.js, all configurable through a single YAML file.

Versioning integrations (via tools like mike) let teams publish and switch between multiple documentation versions, while extensive Markdown extensions add features like tabbed content, task lists and footnotes without writing custom HTML.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Material for MkDocs is entirely free and open source. It previously used a sponsorware model where advanced features were reserved for paying GitHub Sponsors on an Insiders edition starting at 15 US dollars per month.

As of the 9.7.0 release in late 2025, all Insiders features were merged into the free edition, so every capability is now available to all users at no cost, and the project has entered maintenance mode as development shifts to the successor project Zensical.

features.title: Client-side search
features.description: A fast, customizable search engine runs entirely in the browser, including support for searching inside code blocks.

features.title: Instant navigation
features.description: Pages load instantly via client-side routing rather than full page reloads, a feature originally reserved for Insiders.

features.title: Versioned documentation
features.description: Integrates with tools like mike to host and switch between multiple documentation versions.

features.title: Dark and light themes
features.description: Built-in color scheme toggling with extensive palette customization.

features.title: Markdown extensions
features.description: Supports admonitions, content tabs, code annotations, task lists and diagrams via Mermaid.js.

features.title: Responsive, accessible design
features.description: Sites render cleanly across devices and follow accessibility best practices out of the box.

features.title: Social card generation
features.description: Automatically generates social preview images for each documentation page.

features.title: SEO-friendly static output
features.description: Produces static HTML that is easy to host, cache and index by search engines.

pros: Completely free after the 2025 move out of the sponsorware model
Extremely fast, client-side search with no backend service required
Large, mature open-source community and extensive documentation
Highly configurable through a single mkdocs.yml file
Produces clean, professional, SEO-friendly static sites
cons: Now in maintenance mode, with new feature work moving to the successor project Zensical
Requires Python and MkDocs familiarity to set up and customize
No built-in hosting, so teams must deploy the static output themselves
Less visual customization flexibility than a fully custom-built site
Not ideal for non-technical writers without developer support

faqs.question: Is Material for MkDocs free
faqs.answer: Yes. As of the 9.7.0 release, all previously paid Insiders features were merged into the free, open-source edition, so the entire theme is now free to use.

faqs.question: What is the Insiders edition
faqs.answer: Insiders was previously a paid, sponsor-only edition that offered early access to advanced features. It has been discontinued and its features folded into the free release.

faqs.question: Is Material for MkDocs still actively developed
faqs.answer: The project is in maintenance mode, receiving bug and security fixes, while new feature development has moved to the successor project Zensical by the same creator.

faqs.question: Does Material for MkDocs support versioned docs
faqs.answer: Yes, through integration with external tools like mike, which lets teams publish and navigate multiple documentation versions.

faqs.question: Do I need to know Python to use it
faqs.answer: Basic familiarity with Python and pip helps for installation, but day-to-day usage mainly involves writing Markdown and editing a YAML configuration file.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Material for MkDocs
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Docusaurus, GitBook, ReadMe, Sphinx with Read the Docs theming, and the newer Zensical project.

use_cases.title: Open-source project documentation
use_cases.description: Maintainers publish developer guides, API references and tutorials as a fast, searchable static site.

use_cases.title: Internal engineering knowledge bases
use_cases.description: Engineering teams document internal tools, architecture and runbooks in Markdown with versioned releases.

use_cases.title: Product and API documentation
use_cases.description: Companies publish developer-facing product or API docs with search, dark mode and branding.

enrichment.short_description: Mathesar is an open-source, self-hosted spreadsheet-like interface that lets non-technical and technical users view, edit and query PostgreSQL data directly.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); free tier available in early-beta Mathesar Cloud
enrichment.seo_title: Mathesar Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mathesar review 2026: an open-source spreadsheet UI for Postgres. See features, self-hosted and cloud pricing, pros, cons, and alternatives like Airtable.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mathesar is a free, 100 percent open-source, self-hosted user interface that gives spreadsheet-like access to PostgreSQL databases, letting users of any technical skill level view, edit, query and collaborate on relational data without writing SQL. The project began as an effort funded by a grant to explore better tools for working directly with Postgres, and its stewardship now sits with the Mathesar Foundation, a US-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit led by CEO and project lead Kriti Godey. In March 2024 the Mathesar Foundation received a 3 million dollar grant from the Reid Hoffman Foundation to support continued development, and the project is built on Django for its backend and works directly against native Postgres schemas, tables and access control rather than layering its own proprietary data model on top, which distinguishes it from spreadsheet-database hybrids that store data outside the source database. Because Mathesar reads and writes real Postgres tables, any data entered through its interface is immediately usable by other tools, applications and reports that already talk to that same Postgres instance, and Postgres's native roles and permissions carry through into Mathesar's access control. Core features include an Airtable-like grid view for browsing and editing rows, support for complex data types and constraints, the ability to link and explore relationships between tables, saved and shareable data explorations, and multi-user collaboration with granular permissions inherited from Postgres. Deployment is done via Docker or Docker Compose on a user's own infrastructure, keeping the project fully self-hostable, which the Foundation has stated is a permanent commitment. In addition to the self-hosted release, Mathesar Cloud launched as an early-beta hosted offering with a free workspace tier, aimed at users who want to try Mathesar against their data without installing or managing servers. Mathesar targets small businesses, nonprofits, researchers, government teams and internal tool builders who have data trapped in Postgres and want a no-code way to browse and manipulate it without asking a developer to write custom admin panels or SQL queries. Its closest competitors and points of comparison are Airtable, Baserow, NocoDB, and Postgres-specific admin tools like pgAdmin, Retool or Supabase's table editor. Mathesar differentiates itself by combining a fully open-source, self-hostable codebase with native Postgres compatibility (no data migration or proxy layer required), nonprofit governance rather than a venture-backed business model, and a stated mission to make structured data broadly interoperable and portable rather than locked into a proprietary SaaS format.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mathesar gives PostgreSQL databases a spreadsheet-style front end, so people who are comfortable with tools like Excel or Airtable can browse, filter, edit and explore relational data without learning SQL.

The project is stewarded by the Mathesar Foundation, a US nonprofit that received a 3 million dollar grant from the Reid Hoffman Foundation in 2024, and is built to work directly against native Postgres schemas and access controls rather than a separate proprietary data layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users get an Airtable-style grid interface for viewing and editing rows, the ability to explore and link related tables, saved data explorations that can be shared with teammates, and multi-user collaboration governed by Postgres's own role-based permissions.

Because Mathesar operates directly on real Postgres tables rather than importing or syncing data into a separate store, any changes made through the interface are immediately visible to every other application already connected to that same database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The self-hosted edition of Mathesar is completely free and open source under a permissive license, deployable via Docker or Docker Compose on infrastructure the user controls.

Mathesar Cloud is a newer, early-beta hosted option with a free workspace tier for users who want to try the product without managing servers themselves; the Foundation has stated self-hosting will always remain a core, fully supported option.

features.title: Spreadsheet-style data grid
features.description: Browse, sort, filter and edit Postgres table rows through an Airtable-like grid interface.

features.title: Native Postgres compatibility
features.description: Operates directly on existing Postgres schemas and tables with no proprietary data layer or migration required.

features.title: Postgres-native access control
features.description: Permissions inherit from Postgres roles, so database-level security policies carry through to the UI.

features.title: Relationship exploration
features.description: Users can link and navigate related tables to understand and edit connected data.

features.title: Saved data explorations
features.description: Queries and views can be saved and shared with collaborators for repeated use.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs via Docker or Docker Compose on a user's own infrastructure, keeping data fully under their control.

features.title: Multi-user collaboration
features.description: Multiple users can work on the same database concurrently with role-based access.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Fully open source on GitHub, allowing self-hosting, auditing and community contributions.

pros: Completely free and open source with a permanent self-hosting commitment
Works directly on native Postgres data with no proprietary lock-in or data migration
Nonprofit governance backed by a substantial grant, reducing vendor risk
Familiar, low-code spreadsheet interface accessible to non-developers
Access control inherits from existing Postgres roles and permissions
cons: Requires Docker and basic self-hosting know-how for the primary deployment path
Mathesar Cloud is still in early beta with limited maturity
Smaller ecosystem and community than established tools like Airtable or NocoDB
Feature set is narrower than mature no-code database platforms
Best suited to teams already using Postgres rather than other databases

faqs.question: Is Mathesar free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted version of Mathesar is completely free and open source. A hosted Mathesar Cloud option is in early beta and also offers a free tier.

faqs.question: Does Mathesar work with databases other than Postgres
faqs.answer: Mathesar is built specifically for PostgreSQL and works directly with native Postgres schemas, tables and access controls.

faqs.question: Who maintains Mathesar
faqs.answer: Mathesar is maintained by the Mathesar Foundation, a US-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit that received a 3 million dollar grant from the Reid Hoffman Foundation in 2024.

faqs.question: Do I need to know SQL to use Mathesar
faqs.answer: No, Mathesar is designed so users of any technical skill level can view, edit and query data through a spreadsheet-like interface without writing SQL.

faqs.question: How is Mathesar deployed
faqs.answer: The self-hosted version is typically deployed with Docker or Docker Compose on infrastructure the user controls.

faqs.question: How does Mathesar compare to Airtable
faqs.answer: Unlike Airtable, Mathesar is open source, self-hostable, and operates directly on an existing Postgres database rather than a proprietary hosted data store.

use_cases.title: Non-technical data editing on Postgres
use_cases.description: Business or operations staff need to view and edit production Postgres data without SQL access.

use_cases.title: Internal tool replacement
use_cases.description: Teams that would otherwise build a custom admin panel use Mathesar instead to browse and manage relational data.

use_cases.title: Nonprofit and government data management
use_cases.description: Organizations with limited technical resources manage structured data collaboratively without vendor lock-in.

features: Deploy via Docker or Docker Compose

enrichment.short_description: Matomo is an open-source, privacy-focused web analytics platform, formerly known as Piwik, that gives businesses 100 percent ownership of their visitor data.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted Community edition); Cloud plans from around EUR 29/month
enrichment.seo_title: Matomo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Matomo review 2026: the open-source, privacy-first Google Analytics alternative. Compare Cloud and on-premise pricing, features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Matomo is an open-source web analytics platform originally released in 2007 by French developer Matthieu Aubry under the name Piwik, and officially rebranded to Matomo (Maori for 'insight') in January 2018. The company is headquartered in Wellington, New Zealand, and positions itself as the leading privacy-focused alternative to Google Analytics, installed on more than 1 million websites in over 190 countries as of recent counts. Matomo's core differentiator is data ownership: unlike Google Analytics, which processes data on Google's servers and uses it across its advertising ecosystem, Matomo can be self-hosted entirely on a customer's own infrastructure, or run in Matomo's own Cloud with data centers based in Frankfurt, Germany, giving European customers a way to keep analytics data within the EU for GDPR compliance. The platform is built to be a full digital analytics suite rather than just pageview tracking, offering website analytics, heatmaps and session recordings, funnels, A/B testing, form analytics, media analytics, marketing attribution, cohort analysis, tag management and a roll-up reporting dashboard for agencies managing multiple client sites. Because it is open source, Matomo also has a large plugin marketplace maintained by its community and third-party developers, letting users extend core functionality. Pricing follows two parallel tracks: Matomo Cloud, a fully managed SaaS offering starting around 29 euros per month for smaller traffic tiers and scaling with hit volume up to custom Enterprise pricing for very high-traffic sites, and Matomo On-Premise, where the Community edition is free forever for unlimited users and unlimited traffic when self-hosted, while paid on-premise tiers (Team, Business, Enterprise and VIP) add capacity, advanced features like cohorts, attribution and SAML/LDAP authentication, and support, generally priced from a few hundred to a few thousand euros per month depending on scale. Matomo targets privacy-conscious businesses, government agencies, universities, publishers and agencies operating in regulated markets (particularly the EU) who need GDPR- and cookie-law-compliant analytics without ceding data to a third-party ad platform. Its main competitors are Google Analytics 4, Plausible, Fathom Analytics, Adobe Analytics and Mixpanel. Matomo differentiates itself from lightweight privacy analytics tools like Plausible and Fathom by offering a much deeper analytics feature set (heatmaps, session recordings, funnels, A/B testing) comparable to Google Analytics or Adobe Analytics, combined with the option of full self-hosting and open-source auditability that neither Google nor Adobe offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Matomo, originally launched in 2007 as Piwik, is an open-source web analytics platform built around the idea that businesses should own and control their visitor data rather than handing it to a third-party advertising company.

Headquartered in Wellington, New Zealand, Matomo is used on well over a million websites worldwide and is a common choice for organizations that must comply with GDPR or other data-protection regulations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beyond standard traffic and conversion analytics, Matomo includes heatmaps, session recordings, funnels, A/B testing, form and media analytics, marketing attribution, cohort analysis and a built-in tag manager, making it a fuller analytics suite than most privacy-first competitors.
Its open-source plugin marketplace lets users extend the platform, and roll-up reporting allows agencies to view combined analytics across many client sites from a single dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Matomo On-Premise Community edition is free forever for unlimited users and traffic when self-hosted, with paid on-premise tiers (Team, Business, Enterprise, VIP) adding capacity and advanced features starting from roughly 230 euros per month.

Matomo Cloud is a fully managed SaaS option starting around 29 euros per month for lower-traffic sites, scaling up by traffic volume, with Business and Enterprise tiers adding heatmaps, session recording, A/B testing and dedicated support.

features.title: 100 percent data ownership
features.description: Self-hosted or EU-hosted cloud deployment keeps analytics data out of third-party advertising ecosystems.

features.title: Heatmaps and session recordings
features.description: Visualize where visitors click, scroll and move, and replay individual sessions.

features.title: Funnels and A/B testing
features.description: Track multi-step conversion funnels and run experiments directly within the platform.

features.title: Marketing attribution
features.description: Understand which channels and campaigns drive conversions across the customer journey.

features.title: Tag manager
features.description: Deploy and manage tracking tags and scripts without editing site code directly.

features.title: Cohort and roll-up reporting
features.description: Analyze user cohorts over time and aggregate reporting across multiple sites for agencies.

features.title: GDPR-friendly compliance tools
features.description: Built-in features support cookie consent, IP anonymization and data-retention controls.

features.title: Open-source plugin ecosystem
features.description: A marketplace of community and official plugins extends core analytics functionality.

pros: Full data ownership through self-hosting or EU-based cloud hosting
Feature depth (heatmaps, funnels, A/B testing) rivaling paid enterprise analytics suites
Free, unlimited-traffic self-hosted Community edition
Strong GDPR and privacy-regulation compliance posture
Open-source codebase with an extensible plugin marketplace
cons: Self-hosting requires server setup, maintenance and updates
Cloud and on-premise paid tiers can get expensive at high traffic volumes
Interface can feel more complex than lightweight tools like Plausible
Advanced features (heatmaps, session recording, A/B testing) are gated behind paid tiers
Smaller integration ecosystem than Google Analytics

faqs.question: Is Matomo free
faqs.answer: The self-hosted Community edition is free forever with unlimited users and traffic. Matomo Cloud and paid on-premise tiers add hosting, support and advanced features for a fee.

faqs.question: Was Matomo previously called Piwik
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform was founded in 2007 as Piwik and was officially renamed Matomo in January 2018.

faqs.question: Where is Matomo's data hosted for Cloud customers
faqs.answer: Matomo Cloud data is hosted in Frankfurt, Germany, which helps European customers meet GDPR data-residency requirements.

faqs.question: Is Matomo GDPR compliant
faqs.answer: Matomo is built with privacy regulation in mind and includes features like IP anonymization, data-retention controls and self-hosting options that support GDPR compliance.

faqs.question: How does Matomo compare to Google Analytics
faqs.answer: Matomo offers a comparable feature set to Google Analytics but lets organizations retain full ownership of their data through self-hosting, rather than sending data to Google's servers.

faqs.question: Does Matomo offer heatmaps and session recordings
faqs.answer: Yes, heatmaps and session recordings are available on Matomo Cloud's Business tier and on paid on-premise plans.

use_cases.title: GDPR-compliant website analytics
use_cases.description: Businesses operating in the EU need visitor analytics that keeps data ownership and residency compliant with privacy law.

use_cases.title: Agency multi-site reporting
use_cases.description: Digital agencies track analytics across many client websites from a single roll-up dashboard.

use_cases.title: Conversion optimization
use_cases.description: Teams use heatmaps, session recordings, funnels and A/B testing to improve on-site conversion rates.

features: Up to 30 websites and team members

enrichment.short_description: Mattermost is an open-source, self-hostable team messaging and collaboration platform positioned as a secure, customizable alternative to Slack for regulated and technical organizations.
enrichment.seo_title: Mattermost Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mattermost review 2026: the open-source Slack alternative for secure, self-hosted team messaging. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mattermost is an open-source team collaboration and messaging platform founded in 2011 by Corey Hulen and Ian Tien, with the company headquartered in Palo Alto, California. Mattermost, Inc. has raised a total of roughly 70 million dollars across multiple funding rounds, including a Series B in 2019, from investors backing its focus on secure, self-hostable communication for technical, regulated and government organizations. Architecturally, Mattermost is written in Go on the backend with a React-based web client, distributed as a single binary that can be deployed with a PostgreSQL database, which makes self-hosting comparatively lightweight versus other enterprise chat platforms. It ships in three main configurations: a free, source-available Team Edition/Starter tier that is self-hosted and offers unlimited users, channels and message history; a Professional tier priced at 10 dollars per user per month (billed annually) that adds single sign-on, Active Directory/LDAP sync, multi-factor authentication, guest accounts and business-hours support; and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier that adds high-availability clustering, compliance exports, custom admin roles, advanced Elasticsearch-powered search, and a 24x7 support SLA with a 4-hour response time. Both Professional and Enterprise licenses can be run self-hosted or via Mattermost Cloud, giving organizations flexibility between full data control and a managed SaaS experience. Beyond core channel-based messaging, voice calls and screen sharing, Mattermost's signature feature is Playbooks, a workflow automation tool used to build repeatable, checklist-driven processes such as incident response, security operations and structured onboarding, tightly integrated with channels so that a triggered playbook automatically creates a dedicated channel with tasks, owners and status tracking. The platform integrates with development and ITSM tools including GitHub, GitLab, Jenkins, Jira and ServiceNow, and supports custom slash commands, incoming/outgoing webhooks and a bot framework for building internal automation. Mattermost is aimed primarily at software engineering teams, DevOps and incident-response organizations, and public-sector, defense and financial-services customers who require self-hosted or air-gapped deployments for compliance reasons, distinguishing it from cloud-only competitors. Its main competitors are Slack, Microsoft Teams, Rocket.Chat and Zulip. Mattermost differentiates itself through its open-source core, air-gapped and on-premise deployment options, developer-centric integrations, and Playbooks-driven workflow automation, which are especially valued by engineering and incident-response teams and by government or regulated organizations that cannot use cloud-only chat platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mattermost is an open-source alternative to Slack and Microsoft Teams, built for organizations that need full control over where their communication data lives, whether that is a self-hosted server, an air-gapped environment, or Mattermost's managed cloud.

Founded in 2011 by Corey Hulen and Ian Tien and headquartered in Palo Alto, California, Mattermost has built a strong following among software engineering teams, DevOps organizations and government or regulated-industry customers who need secure, self-hostable messaging.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core messaging features include channels, direct and group messages, voice calls and screen sharing, alongside deep integrations with developer tools like GitHub, GitLab, Jenkins, Jira and ServiceNow via webhooks, slash commands and bots.

Playbooks, Mattermost's workflow automation feature, lets teams build repeatable, checklist-driven processes for incident response, security operations or onboarding, automatically spinning up a dedicated channel with tasks and status tracking when triggered.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free Starter tier is self-hosted with unlimited users, channels and message history, making it a capable entry point for small teams or evaluation.

Professional costs 10 dollars per user per month billed annually and adds SSO, LDAP sync, MFA and guest accounts, while Enterprise is custom-priced and adds high-availability clustering, compliance exports and 24x7 support, available either self-hosted or via Mattermost Cloud.

features.title: Channel-based messaging
features.description: Organize conversations into public, private and direct-message channels, similar to Slack.

features.title: Playbooks workflow automation
features.description: Build repeatable, checklist-driven processes for incident response, onboarding or other multi-step workflows.

features.title: Self-hosted and air-gapped deployment
features.description: Runs as a single Go binary with PostgreSQL, supporting fully on-premise or air-gapped installations.

features.title: Voice calls and screen sharing
features.description: Built-in calling and screen-share tools reduce the need for separate video conferencing apps.

features.title: Developer tool integrations
features.description: Native integrations with GitHub, GitLab, Jenkins, Jira and ServiceNow plus custom webhooks and bots.

features.title: SSO and directory sync
features.description: Professional and Enterprise tiers support SAML/OAuth SSO and Active Directory/LDAP synchronization.

features.title: Compliance exports and audit logging
features.description: Enterprise tier adds compliance-grade message export and advanced administrative controls.

features.title: High-availability clustering
features.description: Enterprise deployments can scale across clustered nodes for reliability at large organizations.

pros: Fully open-source core with self-hosted and air-gapped deployment options
Strong fit for regulated industries, government and defense customers
Playbooks provide built-in workflow automation not native to Slack
Deep integrations with developer and DevOps tooling
Lightweight architecture (single Go binary) makes self-hosting comparatively simple
cons: Smaller app/integration marketplace than Slack or Microsoft Teams
Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted and not publicly transparent
Self-hosting requires ongoing server maintenance and updates
UI and mobile experience is generally considered less polished than Slack
Annual prepaid billing only for official Professional and Enterprise licenses, no monthly option

faqs.question: Is Mattermost free
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter tier is free and self-hosted with unlimited users, channels and message history. Paid Professional and Enterprise tiers add security, compliance and support features.

faqs.question: Can Mattermost be self-hosted
faqs.answer: Yes, Mattermost can be fully self-hosted, including in air-gapped environments, or run through Mattermost Cloud as a managed service.

faqs.question: How much does Mattermost Professional cost
faqs.answer: Professional costs 10 dollars per user per month, billed annually.

faqs.question: What is Mattermost Playbooks
faqs.answer: Playbooks is a workflow automation feature for building repeatable, checklist-driven processes like incident response or onboarding, integrated directly with channels.

faqs.question: Who uses Mattermost
faqs.answer: Mattermost is popular with software engineering and DevOps teams, as well as government, defense and financial-services organizations that need self-hosted or compliant messaging.

faqs.question: How does Mattermost compare to Slack
faqs.answer: Mattermost offers similar core messaging features to Slack but adds open-source self-hosting, air-gapped deployment and built-in workflow automation, targeting security- and compliance-sensitive organizations.

use_cases.title: Incident response and DevOps workflows
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use Playbooks to coordinate incident response with automated checklists and dedicated channels.

use_cases.title: Regulated and government communication
use_cases.description: Public-sector and financial-services organizations need self-hosted or air-gapped messaging to meet compliance requirements.

use_cases.title: Secure internal team chat
use_cases.description: Organizations that want Slack-like messaging while keeping full control of where data is stored.

enrichment.short_description: Mautic is an open-source marketing automation platform, now owned by Acquia, offering email marketing, campaign automation, forms and segmentation for free self-hosting or paid cloud plans.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted, open source); Mautic Cloud from roughly EUR 247.50/month
enrichment.seo_title: Mautic Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mautic review 2026: the open-source marketing automation platform owned by Acquia. Compare self-hosted vs cloud pricing, features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mautic is an open-source marketing automation platform originally built in 2014 by David Hurley and a small team of developers under a company called Allyde, which rebranded to Mautic, Inc. in 2016. Mautic quickly became the largest open-source player in marketing automation, with more than 200,000 installations and, according to the company, over 250,000 users across roughly 188 countries. On May 8, 2019, Acquia, the digital experience platform company co-founded by Drupal creator Dries Buytaert, announced it had acquired Mautic to build what it described as the world's first open marketing cloud, integrating Mautic's marketing automation with Acquia's Drupal-based content management and Acquia's broader digital experience platform; financial terms of the acquisition were not disclosed. Under Acquia, Mautic continues to be developed as an open-source project (Mautic Open Source), while Acquia also offers a fully managed Mautic Cloud product for organizations that don't want to self-host. Architecturally, Mautic is a PHP-based application (built on the Symfony framework) that tracks contact behavior across email, web, forms and other channels, then triggers automated marketing campaigns and journeys based on that behavior. Core features include a drag-and-drop email builder with A/B testing and personalization tokens, a visual campaign builder supporting branching decision trees, conditions and time-based delays, dynamic contact segmentation that updates automatically based on behavior and profile data, an embeddable form builder with progressive profiling, landing page creation, lead scoring, and integrations with CRM and other marketing tools. Because it is open source, Mautic Open Source is free to download and self-host, with the only costs being infrastructure, an SMTP/email-sending provider, and setup or maintenance time or services. Mautic Cloud, the managed SaaS option, is tiered by contact volume and sending capacity: an Essential tier around 247.50 euros per month covering roughly 50,000 emails and 50,000 contacts, a Professional tier starting around 1,237 euros per month with dedicated IP and advanced automation scaling to millions of contacts, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier for large-scale sending with dedicated infrastructure. Mautic targets mid-market and enterprise marketing teams, agencies and organizations that want the depth of platforms like Marketo, HubSpot or Salesforce Marketing Cloud without the associated licensing costs, particularly those already invested in Drupal or open-source infrastructure. Its main competitors are HubSpot, Marketo (Adobe), Salesforce Marketing Cloud, ActiveCampaign and Klaviyo. Mautic differentiates itself as essentially the only enterprise-grade, fully open-source marketing automation platform, giving technical teams complete customization and data control that closed SaaS competitors cannot match.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mautic is an open-source marketing automation platform that lets organizations build email campaigns, automated customer journeys, landing pages and lead-scoring workflows without the licensing costs of closed platforms like Marketo or HubSpot.

Founded in 2014 and acquired by digital experience platform company Acquia in 2019, Mautic remains available as a free, self-hosted open-source project alongside a managed Mautic Cloud offering from Acquia.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mautic's drag-and-drop campaign builder supports branching decision trees, conditions and time-based delays, letting marketers build multi-step nurture journeys triggered by contact behavior or form submissions.

A drag-and-drop email builder supports personalization tokens, dynamic content and A/B testing, while dynamic segmentation automatically updates contact lists based on behavior and profile data, and an embeddable form builder supports progressive profiling for gradual lead capture.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mautic Open Source is free to self-host under an open-source license; the only costs are hosting infrastructure, an SMTP or email-sending provider, and setup and maintenance effort.

Mautic Cloud is a managed SaaS option with tiers starting around 247.50 euros per month for the Essential tier (roughly 50,000 emails and contacts), scaling to a Professional tier from around 1,237 euros per month and a custom-priced Enterprise tier for very large sending volumes.

features.title: Drag-and-drop email builder
features.description: Design personalized, A/B-testable emails with dynamic content and merge tokens.

features.title: Visual campaign builder
features.description: Build multi-step automated journeys with branching logic, conditions and time-based delays.

features.title: Dynamic segmentation
features.description: Automatically group contacts based on behavior and profile data, with support for nested segments.

features.title: Form builder with progressive profiling
features.description: Embed forms on pages or in popups and gradually collect more contact data over multiple visits.

features.title: Landing pages
features.description: Build and publish landing pages tied directly into campaign workflows.

features.title: Lead scoring
features.description: Assign scores to contacts based on engagement and behavior to prioritize sales follow-up.

features.title: Open-source self-hosting
features.description: Fully free, self-hostable core platform with an active plugin and integration ecosystem.

features.title: Mautic Cloud managed hosting
features.description: Acquia-managed SaaS tiers for teams that want Mautic without self-hosting infrastructure.

pros: Free, fully open-source core with no licensing fees for self-hosting
Enterprise-grade automation depth rivaling paid platforms like Marketo
Backed by Acquia, giving it stability and integration with Drupal's digital experience platform
Large global installed base and active open-source community
Highly customizable due to open codebase and extensive plugin ecosystem
cons: Self-hosting requires infrastructure setup, maintenance and a separate SMTP provider
Cloud pricing rises quickly at higher contact and sending volumes
Steeper learning curve than simpler tools like Mailchimp or ActiveCampaign
UI can feel dated compared to newer marketing automation platforms
Advanced deliverability and support require paid Cloud tiers or third-party services

faqs.question: Is Mautic free
faqs.answer: Mautic Open Source is free to self-host. Acquia also offers a paid, fully managed Mautic Cloud starting around 247.50 euros per month.

faqs.question: Who owns Mautic
faqs.answer: Mautic is owned by Acquia, the digital experience platform company, which acquired Mautic in May 2019.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Mautic
faqs.answer: Yes, Mautic Open Source can be self-hosted on your own infrastructure at no licensing cost, though you pay for hosting and email-sending services.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Mautic Open Source and Mautic Cloud
faqs.answer: Mautic Open Source is the free, self-hosted version, while Mautic Cloud is a fully managed, paid SaaS version hosted by Acquia with tiered pricing based on contacts and email volume.

faqs.question: Does Mautic support marketing automation and lead scoring
faqs.answer: Yes, Mautic includes a visual campaign builder for automated journeys and lead scoring based on contact engagement and behavior.

faqs.question: How does Mautic compare to HubSpot
faqs.answer: Mautic offers comparable automation depth to HubSpot but as an open-source platform, giving organizations full data control and no per-contact licensing fees when self-hosted.

use_cases.title: Cost-effective enterprise marketing automation
use_cases.description: Mid-market and enterprise teams need Marketo- or HubSpot-level automation without high per-contact licensing costs.

use_cases.title: Drupal-based digital experience marketing
use_cases.description: Organizations already using Acquia's Drupal-based digital experience platform integrate Mautic for unified marketing.

use_cases.title: Data-sovereign marketing automation
use_cases.description: Organizations that must keep contact and campaign data on their own infrastructure self-host Mautic.

enrichment.short_description: Maxio is a subscription billing and financial operations platform for B2B SaaS companies, formed from the 2022 merger of Chargify and SaaSOptics.
enrichment.headquarters: Atlanta, Georgia and San Antonio, Texas, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Maxio Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Maxio review 2026: subscription billing and finance platform for B2B SaaS, formed from Chargify and SaaSOptics. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Maxio is a subscription billing, revenue recognition and financial operations platform built specifically for business-to-business SaaS companies. It was formed in April 2022 through the merger of two established SaaS finance tools: Chargify, a subscription billing and invoicing platform founded in 2009 and based in San Antonio, Texas, and SaaSOptics, a subscription finance and revenue-recognition platform also founded in 2009 and based in Atlanta, Georgia. The merger followed a combined 150 million dollar growth-equity investment from Battery Ventures in April 2021, and the newly combined company rebranded as Maxio, maintaining offices in San Antonio, Atlanta, Dublin (Ireland) and Krakow (Poland). At the time of the merger, the combined business served more than 2,300 customers worldwide and managed over 10 billion dollars in customer annual recurring revenue. Maxio's product today is split into two connected pillars: Advanced Billing (the former Chargify product), which handles complex, usage-based and hybrid subscription billing, invoicing, dunning and payment processing; and Advanced Revenue Management (the former SaaSOptics product), which automates GAAP- and IFRS-compliant revenue recognition, accounts-receivable management, expense amortization and SaaS metrics reporting (MRR, ARR, churn, cohort analysis) for finance teams and investors. The platform is built to serve the full order-to-cash and finance-reporting workflow for subscription businesses, integrating with accounting systems like QuickBooks and NetSuite, CRMs like Salesforce and HubSpot, and payment processors. Maxio's pricing is structured around three tiers: a Build plan offering a free 30-day sandbox for developers to test billing logic, a Grow plan priced at 599 dollars per month flat (no per-seat charges) for companies billing up to 100,000 dollars per month, and a Scale plan, quote-only, for companies billing above that threshold, which adds advanced revenue recognition, accounts-receivable management, expense amortization, multi-entity support and usage-based metering and rating as optional add-on modules. An Enterprise tier with fully custom pricing is also available for organizations needing maximum capabilities, unlimited usage, advanced security and dedicated support. Maxio's target customers are venture-backed and private-equity-backed B2B SaaS companies, typically post-seed through growth stage, that have outgrown simple payment processors like Stripe Billing and need dedicated subscription billing plus investor-grade revenue recognition and SaaS metrics. Its main competitors include Chargebee, Zuora, Recurly, Stripe Billing and Chargify's legacy standalone positioning. Maxio differentiates itself by combining billing and finance/revenue-recognition capabilities that are usually sold as two separate products, giving finance teams a single source of truth from invoice generation through GAAP-compliant revenue reporting and investor metrics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Maxio is a subscription billing and finance platform purpose-built for B2B SaaS companies, combining invoicing and usage-based billing with revenue recognition and SaaS metrics reporting in one connected system.

The company was formed in April 2022 when Chargify, a subscription billing platform, merged with SaaSOptics, a subscription finance and revenue-recognition platform; both companies were founded in 2009 and had received a combined 150 million dollar investment from Battery Ventures in 2021 prior to the merger.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Advanced Billing (formerly Chargify) handles complex and usage-based subscription billing, invoicing, dunning and payment processing, while Advanced Revenue Management (formerly SaaSOptics) automates GAAP- and IFRS-compliant revenue recognition, accounts-receivable management and SaaS metrics like MRR, ARR and churn.

The platform integrates with accounting systems such as QuickBooks and NetSuite and CRMs such as Salesforce and HubSpot, giving finance teams an end-to-end view from invoice generation to investor-ready revenue reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Maxio's Grow plan costs 599 dollars per month flat, with no per-seat charges, for companies billing up to 100,000 dollars per month and includes the standard billing feature set.

Companies billing above that threshold move to the quote-only Scale plan, which adds optional modules for advanced revenue recognition, accounts-receivable management, multi-entity support and usage-based metering, while a custom-priced Enterprise tier serves organizations needing maximum scale and dedicated support.

features.title: Usage-based and hybrid billing
features.description: Supports flat-rate, usage-based, tiered and hybrid subscription pricing models.

features.title: Automated revenue recognition
features.description: GAAP- and IFRS-compliant revenue recognition automation for finance and audit teams.

features.title: SaaS metrics reporting
features.description: Tracks MRR, ARR, churn, cohort analysis and other investor-facing subscription metrics.

features.title: Accounts-receivable management
features.description: Automates invoicing, dunning and collections workflows to reduce manual AR work.

features.title: Multi-entity support
features.description: Manages billing and revenue recognition across multiple business entities or brands.

features.title: Accounting and CRM integrations
features.description: Connects with QuickBooks, NetSuite, Salesforce, HubSpot and other finance and sales tools.

features.title: Developer sandbox
features.description: A free 30-day Build plan lets developers test billing logic before going live.

features.title: Expense amortization
features.description: Automates amortization of sales commissions and other deferred expenses for SaaS finance teams.

pros: Combines subscription billing and revenue recognition in one connected platform
Strong fit for complex, usage-based and hybrid B2B SaaS pricing models
GAAP/IFRS-compliant revenue automation valuable for audits and investor reporting
Flat-rate Grow plan avoids per-seat pricing for growing companies
Backed by a well-funded, established company with a large existing customer base
cons: Enterprise and Scale pricing is not publicly transparent and requires a custom quote
More complex to implement than simple billing tools like Stripe Billing
Primarily built for B2B SaaS, less suited to B2C or non-subscription businesses
Advanced revenue management features are add-on modules on higher tiers
Migration from legacy Chargify or SaaSOptics accounts can involve a learning curve

faqs.question: What companies merged to form Maxio
faqs.answer: Maxio was formed in April 2022 from the merger of Chargify, a subscription billing platform, and SaaSOptics, a subscription finance and revenue-recognition platform, both founded in 2009.

faqs.question: How much does Maxio cost
faqs.answer: Maxio's Grow plan costs 599 dollars per month flat for companies billing up to 100,000 dollars per month. Companies above that threshold move to the quote-only Scale or Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: Does Maxio handle revenue recognition
faqs.answer: Yes, Maxio's Advanced Revenue Management module (formerly SaaSOptics) automates GAAP- and IFRS-compliant revenue recognition and SaaS metrics reporting.

faqs.question: Who is Maxio built for
faqs.answer: Maxio is built for B2B SaaS companies, particularly venture-backed and private-equity-backed businesses that need subscription billing plus investor-grade financial reporting.

faqs.question: Can I try Maxio for free
faqs.answer: Maxio offers a Build plan with a free 30-day sandbox for developers to test billing logic before committing to a paid plan.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Maxio
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Chargebee, Zuora, Recurly and Stripe Billing.

use_cases.description: Venture-backed SaaS companies need GAAP-compliant revenue recognition and SaaS metrics for board and investor reporting.

enrichment.short_description: Mayan EDMS is a free, open-source, Django-based document management system offering OCR, metadata indexing, workflow automation and electronic signature verification.
enrichment.seo_title: Mayan EDMS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mayan EDMS review 2026: an open-source, Django-based document management system with OCR and workflow automation. See features, pricing, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mayan EDMS is a free and open-source electronic document management system (EDMS) originally created in 2010 by Roberto Rosario, a software developer and former Director of Software Development for the Government of Puerto Rico, who built the tool to help government agencies manage large volumes of permit and regulatory documents at a time when few open-source document management tools existed. The project is built with Python and Django and is designed to scale from small deployments to enterprise-scale repositories handling hundreds of millions of documents, typically deployed via Docker, Docker Compose or Kubernetes. Over more than a decade of continuous development, Mayan EDMS has accumulated over 200 releases and tens of thousands of commits from more than 100 contributors, making it one of the most mature open-source document management platforms available. Core functionality centers on ingesting, indexing, organizing and retrieving documents: the system supports custom metadata fields, color-coded tags, and cabinets (a folder-like organizational structure) so documents can be classified along multiple dimensions simultaneously rather than a single rigid hierarchy. Built-in Optical Character Recognition, powered by the Tesseract engine, extracts and indexes text from scanned documents and images in over 100 languages, and OCR processing can be distributed across multiple worker nodes for large-scale deployments. Mayan EDMS also includes document versioning, full-text search, an advanced workflow engine that tracks document state changes and can trigger automated actions as documents move through business processes, and electronic/digital signature capabilities, including verification of embedded cryptographic signatures and support for uploading detached signature files. More recent releases have added the ability to use large language model output (including OpenAI models) to help drive and automate the workflow engine for more agentic document processing. Mayan EDMS is released under the Apache 2.0 license, making it free to use, modify and redistribute without licensing fees; Roberto Rosario also offers commercial consulting, customization and support services for organizations that need paid assistance. The platform targets organizations that need to digitize and manage large volumes of documents with strict audit, retention and compliance requirements, including government agencies, legal and healthcare organizations, and any business replacing paper-based recordkeeping. Its main competitors include other open-source document management systems like Paperless-ngx and OpenKM, as well as commercial platforms like DocuWare, M-Files and SharePoint. Mayan EDMS differentiates itself through its combination of enterprise-scale architecture, built-in multi-language OCR, a flexible metadata-and-cabinet organizational model instead of rigid folders, and a fully open-source, self-hosted deployment model that avoids per-user or per-document licensing costs entirely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mayan EDMS is an open-source document management system built to help organizations digitize, index and retrieve large volumes of documents, originally created in 2010 by developer Roberto Rosario to help government agencies handle high volumes of permit and regulatory paperwork.

Built on Python and Django, Mayan EDMS has matured over more than a decade into an enterprise-capable platform that can scale from a small self-hosted install to repositories with hundreds of millions of documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Documents can be organized using custom metadata fields, color-coded tags and folder-like cabinets rather than a single rigid hierarchy, and built-in OCR powered by Tesseract extracts searchable text from scanned files in over 100 languages, with processing distributable across multiple worker nodes.

An advanced workflow engine tracks document state changes and can trigger automated actions as documents move through business processes, while electronic signature support allows verification of embedded cryptographic signatures and upload of detached signature files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mayan EDMS is released under the Apache 2.0 open-source license, making it completely free to download, self-host, modify and redistribute with no licensing fees.
Commercial consulting, customization and support services are available from the project's creator for organizations that need paid implementation help, but there is no separate paid software tier.

features.title: Built-in OCR
features.description: Tesseract-powered optical character recognition extracts searchable text from scanned documents in over 100 languages.

features.title: Metadata and cabinets
features.description: Documents can be classified with custom metadata fields, tags and cabinet-style folders along multiple dimensions.

features.title: Document versioning
features.description: Tracks and preserves historical versions of documents as they are updated.

features.title: Workflow automation engine
features.description: Automates business processes by tracking document state and triggering actions on state changes.

features.title: Electronic signature verification
features.description: Verifies embedded cryptographic signatures and supports uploading detached signature files.

features.title: Distributed OCR processing
features.description: OCR workloads can be distributed across multiple servers for large-scale deployments.

features.title: Container-native deployment
features.description: Deploys via Docker, Docker Compose or Kubernetes for scalable, repeatable installs.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Searches across document content and metadata to quickly retrieve relevant files.

pros: Completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license
Enterprise-scale architecture capable of handling hundreds of millions of documents
Built-in multi-language OCR with distributed processing for large repositories
Flexible metadata and cabinet organization instead of rigid folder hierarchies
Mature project with over a decade of continuous development and a large contributor base
cons: Requires self-hosting and Django/Docker familiarity to deploy and maintain
No official managed cloud/SaaS offering from the core project
User interface is less polished than commercial DMS platforms like M-Files or DocuWare
Documentation and support rely heavily on community forums outside of paid consulting
Initial setup and configuration can be complex for non-technical teams

faqs.question: Is Mayan EDMS free
faqs.answer: Yes, Mayan EDMS is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no licensing fees for self-hosted use.

faqs.question: Who created Mayan EDMS
faqs.answer: Mayan EDMS was created in 2010 by Roberto Rosario, a software developer and former Director of Software Development for the Government of Puerto Rico.

faqs.question: Does Mayan EDMS support OCR
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes built-in OCR powered by the Tesseract engine, supporting text extraction in over 100 languages, with processing that can be distributed across multiple servers.

faqs.question: Can Mayan EDMS automate document workflows
faqs.answer: Yes, its workflow engine tracks document state changes and can trigger automated actions as documents move through defined business processes.

faqs.question: How is Mayan EDMS deployed
faqs.answer: It is typically deployed using Docker, Docker Compose or Kubernetes, and is built on Python and Django.

faqs.question: Is commercial support available for Mayan EDMS
faqs.answer: Yes, commercial consulting, customization and support services are available from the project's creator for organizations that need paid assistance.

use_cases.title: Government document digitization
use_cases.description: Public-sector agencies digitize and manage permit, regulatory and case files with strict audit requirements.

use_cases.title: Regulated document retention
use_cases.description: Legal, healthcare and financial organizations need searchable, versioned document storage with electronic signature verification.

use_cases.title: Enterprise-scale archive replacement
use_cases.description: Organizations replacing paper archives or legacy DMS systems need a scalable, self-hosted, cost-free platform.

features: Self-hosted via Docker, Docker Compose or Kubernetes
Direct support from the project's creator
